author: lOrm
content: Reviewed claim — not a bug. rest.find("---") returns the BYTE OFFSET of the start of "---", which is 3 ASCII bytes (0x2D). The bytes at positions end, end+1, end+2 are therefore ALL ASCII dashes; end+3 lands on the byte immediately after the last dash, which is a valid UTF-8 char boundary by construction (the next codepoint MUST start there). Verified with rustc: parsing `---\n吉\n---𠮷` succeeds with body="𠮷". The QA report's scenario "find(\"---\") byte offset sits before non-ASCII content, splitting a codepoint" is impossible: find returns the position WHERE the matched substring begins, and the matched substring is itself 3 ASCII bytes. The only way end+3 could split a codepoint is if find returned an offset inside a multi-byte char, but Rust's str::find guarantees char-boundary offsets. Closing as not-a-bug — no code change warranted.
